case_name: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane removal from well water for municipal drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing 1,2,3-trichloropropane from contaminated well water intended for drinking water use.

input_data.scenario_background: A small community water supplier relies on a groundwater well impacted by 1,2,3-trichloropropane likely associated with historical agricultural chemical use. The well otherwise has relatively good raw water quality and the utility wants a reliable treatment approach that can be operated consistently by staff experienced with standard packaged systems. The main objective is compliance for drinking water without adopting unnecessarily energy-intensive or complex processes.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve compliance with applicable drinking water requirements for 1,2,3-trichloropropane and provide stable potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use proven treatment technology for trace VOC removal
limit energy demand to a moderate level
avoid creating difficult residuals streams where possible

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: remove incidental particulates and protect downstream adsorber performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb 1,2,3-trichloropropane from well water using a proven VOC control process
core_unit_functions.lead_lag_gac_polishing: provide redundancy, improve breakthrough control, and support stable compliance at trace concentrations
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: 1,2,3-trichloropropane is a trace organic contaminant for which activated carbon is a well-established drinking water treatment option
the raw well water has low turbidity and low DOC, which supports good GAC performance and longer media life
lead-lag operation is commonly used for stable removal of trace organics and fits the requirement for reliable municipal operation
this process has moderate energy demand and avoids the high-pressure operation associated with membrane systems
potential_risks: breakthrough can occur if EBCT and media replacement are not managed carefully
competitive adsorption from unexpected organic loading could shorten carbon life
spent carbon handling and reactivation logistics are required

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: transfer volatile organic contaminants from water to air
core_unit_functions.off_gas_treatment: capture or control stripped contaminants before air discharge
core_unit_functions.post_gac_polishing: provide additional polishing and compliance assurance for residual 1,2,3-trichloropropane
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in distributed water

applicability_rationale: air stripping is a recognized technology class for VOC treatment in groundwater applications
it can be effective where a volatile organic compound is the primary issue and where stable packed tower operation is feasible
post-GAC polishing improves confidence for trace-level compliance and addresses variability
this option is technically credible but somewhat more operationally involved than direct GAC adsorption
potential_risks: 1,2,3-trichloropropane may require careful design and sufficient air-to-water ratio due to its physical properties
off-gas permitting and treatment add complexity
tower scaling or fouling can increase maintenance burden

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: protect adsorptive media from particulate loading
core_unit_functions.synthetic_adsorptive_media: remove 1,2,3-trichloropropane using specialized sorptive media for organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.post_gac_or_guard_bed: capture residual breakthrough and provide treatment redundancy
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure finished water remains microbiologically safe

applicability_rationale: specialized adsorptive media may offer targeted treatment for trace organic contaminants in clean groundwater
the well water quality is favorable for fixed-bed adsorption processes
this approach can fit moderate footprint and moderate energy constraints
it ranks below standard GAC because GAC has broader conventional acceptance and more established municipal drinking water track record
potential_risks: site-specific performance may depend strongly on vendor design assumptions and bed life estimates
replacement media supply and disposal pathways must be confirmed
less standardization than GAC may reduce operator familiarity

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: generate reactive radicals to transform 1,2,3-trichloropropane and related organics
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_or_gac_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and provide polishing

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can address recalcitrant organic contaminants where oxidation chemistry is favorable
the process may be considered if additional organic contaminant control is later needed
it is technically feasible but not the most straightforward first choice for a single-contaminant well water case with moderate constraints
potential_risks: treatment reliability for 1,2,3-trichloropropane can depend on oxidant dose, contact conditions, and reactor design
formation of transformation byproducts would require evaluation
chemical handling and process control are more demanding than adsorption

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad contaminant rejection through high-pressure membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for corrosion control and distribution stability
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological protection in finished water

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis can reduce a wide range of contaminants and may be technically capable of lowering 1,2,3-trichloropropane concentrations
it may be considered if the utility later faces multiple dissolved contaminant issues beyond the current single VOC problem
for this case it is not the preferred option because the source water is otherwise relatively clean and the constraints favor simpler proven VOC-specific treatment
potential_risks: high energy use and complex operation are not well aligned with the stated utility needs
concentrate management creates a difficult residual stream
finished water stabilization and membrane fouling control add unnecessary complexity for this case
